name: Structure and functions of the nervous and endocrine systems and ways these systems coordinate the organ systems

nodes.label: The Neuron
nodes.summary: The neuron is the excitable cell of the nervous system specialized for electrical and chemical signaling.

nodes.label: Neuronal Structure
nodes.summary: Neurons have a soma, dendrites, axon, and terminals specialized for receiving and transmitting signals.

nodes.label: Cell body (soma)
nodes.summary: The soma contains the nucleus and organelles and integrates incoming signals at the axon hillock.

nodes.label: Dendrites
nodes.summary: Dendrites are branched processes that receive incoming signals and conduct graded potentials toward the soma.

nodes.label: Axon
nodes.summary: The axon is a single long process that conducts action potentials away from the soma toward the terminals.

nodes.label: Axon hillock
nodes.summary: The axon hillock is the trigger zone where summed potentials reaching threshold initiate the action potential.

nodes.label: Axon terminal / synaptic bouton
nodes.summary: The axon terminal releases neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft upon arrival of an action potential.

nodes.label: Nissl bodies
nodes.summary: Nissl bodies are rough ER aggregates in the soma and dendrites that synthesize neuronal proteins.

nodes.label: Axonal transport (kinesin/dynein)
nodes.summary: Anterograde transport uses kinesin and retrograde transport uses dynein along microtubules in the axon.

nodes.label: Synaptic vesicles
nodes.summary: Synaptic vesicles in the terminal store neurotransmitter and dock at the active zone for calcium-triggered release.

nodes.label: Synapse anatomy (pre/postsynaptic, cleft)
nodes.summary: A synapse comprises a presynaptic terminal, a synaptic cleft, and a postsynaptic membrane bearing receptors.

nodes.label: Neuron Classification
nodes.summary: Neurons are classified by function and structure into sensory, motor, and interneurons.

nodes.label: Sensory (afferent) neurons
nodes.summary: Sensory neurons carry signals from receptors toward the central nervous system.

nodes.label: Motor (efferent) neurons
nodes.summary: Motor neurons carry commands from the CNS to effectors such as muscles and glands.

nodes.label: Interneurons
nodes.summary: Interneurons lie within the CNS and connect sensory and motor pathways, mediating integration.

nodes.label: Structural classes (multipolar, bipolar, unipolar)
nodes.summary: Neurons are also classified structurally by the number of processes as multipolar, bipolar, or pseudounipolar.

nodes.label: Glial Cells
nodes.summary: Glia support, insulate, and protect neurons without conducting action potentials.

nodes.label: Oligodendrocytes
nodes.summary: Oligodendrocytes myelinate multiple axons in the central nervous system.

nodes.label: Schwann cells
nodes.summary: Schwann cells myelinate a single segment of one axon in the peripheral nervous system.

nodes.label: Astrocytes
nodes.summary: Astrocytes regulate the extracellular environment, support the blood-brain barrier, and recycle neurotransmitter.

nodes.label: Microglia
nodes.summary: Microglia are the resident immune phagocytes of the central nervous system.

nodes.label: Ependymal cells
nodes.summary: Ependymal cells line the ventricles and produce cerebrospinal fluid.

nodes.label: Satellite cells
nodes.summary: Satellite cells surround and support neuronal cell bodies in peripheral ganglia.

nodes.label: Myelin sheath
nodes.summary: Myelin is a lipid-rich insulating wrapping that increases axonal conduction velocity.

nodes.label: Myelin as electrical insulation
nodes.summary: Myelin increases membrane resistance and lowers capacitance, preventing current leak between nodes.

nodes.label: Nodes of Ranvier
nodes.summary: Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in myelin where voltage-gated channels cluster to enable saltatory conduction.

nodes.label: Electrochemical Signaling
nodes.summary: Neurons transmit information via membrane potentials and synaptic transmission.

nodes.label: Resting Membrane Potential
nodes.summary: The resting potential is the unstimulated voltage difference across the neuronal membrane.

nodes.label: Resting potential (~-70 mV)
nodes.summary: A typical neuron rests at approximately -70 mV, negative inside relative to outside.

nodes.label: Na+/K+-ATPase
nodes.summary: The Na+/K+-ATPase pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP to maintain ion gradients.

nodes.label: K+ leak channels and selective permeability
nodes.summary: Greater resting permeability to K+ drives the membrane potential near the K+ equilibrium potential.

nodes.label: Ion concentration gradients (Na+, K+, Cl-, Ca2+)
nodes.summary: Na+, Cl-, and Ca2+ are concentrated outside while K+ is concentrated inside the neuron at rest.

nodes.label: Nernst / equilibrium potential
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential at which an ion's electrical and chemical forces balance.

nodes.label: Goldman equation (multi-ion potential)
nodes.summary: The Goldman equation predicts membrane potential from the relative permeabilities of multiple ions.

nodes.label: Action Potential
nodes.summary: The action potential is an all-or-none rapid depolarization that propagates along the axon.

nodes.label: Threshold and all-or-none
nodes.summary: Reaching threshold (~-55 mV) triggers a full action potential whose amplitude is independent of stimulus strength.

nodes.label: Depolarization (Na+ influx)
nodes.summary: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open, allowing Na+ influx that rapidly depolarizes the membrane.

nodes.label: Repolarization (K+ efflux)
nodes.summary: Na+ channels inactivate and voltage-gated K+ channels open, allowing K+ efflux that repolarizes the membrane.

nodes.label: Hyperpolarization (undershoot)
nodes.summary: Slow closing of K+ channels transiently overshoots the resting potential before recovery.

nodes.label: Refractory periods
nodes.summary: The absolute refractory period prevents a new AP while Na+ channels are inactivated; the relative period requires a stronger stimulus.

nodes.label: Voltage-gated Na+ channel states
nodes.summary: Voltage-gated Na+ channels cycle through closed, open, and inactivated states governing AP timing.

nodes.label: Voltage-gated K+ channels (delayed)
nodes.summary: Delayed voltage-gated K+ channels open slowly to drive repolarization and the undershoot.

nodes.label: Frequency (rate) coding of intensity
nodes.summary: Because APs are all-or-none, stimulus intensity is encoded by action potential frequency, not amplitude.

nodes.label: Conduction and Propagation
nodes.summary: The action potential propagates unidirectionally along the axon at speeds set by myelination and diameter.

nodes.label: Saltatory conduction
nodes.summary: In myelinated axons the impulse jumps node to node, greatly speeding conduction.

nodes.label: Determinants of conduction velocity
nodes.summary: Greater myelination and larger axon diameter increase conduction velocity.

nodes.label: Unidirectional propagation
nodes.summary: The refractory period behind the impulse ensures the action potential moves in one direction.

nodes.label: Graded potentials and summation
nodes.summary: Graded potentials vary with stimulus strength and undergo temporal and spatial summation at the hillock.

nodes.label: Temporal vs spatial summation
nodes.summary: Temporal summation adds inputs over time from one synapse; spatial summation adds simultaneous inputs from many synapses.

nodes.label: The Synapse
nodes.summary: The synapse is the junction where one neuron communicates with a target cell.

nodes.label: Chemical synapse mechanism
nodes.summary: AP arrival opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, triggering vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release into the cleft.

nodes.label: Ca2+-triggered vesicle exocytosis
nodes.summary: Calcium influx at the terminal drives synaptic vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter exocytosis.

nodes.label: Postsynaptic receptor binding
nodes.summary: Neurotransmitter binds ionotropic or metabotropic receptors to alter postsynaptic membrane potential.

nodes.label: Ionotropic (ligand-gated) receptors
nodes.summary: Ionotropic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels producing fast, brief postsynaptic responses.

nodes.label: Metabotropic (GPCR) receptors
nodes.summary: Metabotropic receptors act through G proteins and second messengers for slower, longer-lasting effects.

nodes.label: EPSPs and IPSPs
nodes.summary: Excitatory postsynaptic potentials depolarize and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials hyperpolarize the target.

nodes.label: Signal termination (reuptake/degradation)
nodes.summary: Neurotransmitter action ends by reuptake, enzymatic degradation, or diffusion out of the cleft.

nodes.label: Electrical synapses (gap junctions)
nodes.summary: Electrical synapses pass current directly through gap junctions for fast, synchronized signaling.

nodes.label: Neuromuscular junction
nodes.summary: At the neuromuscular junction ACh from a motor neuron triggers an end-plate potential that excites the muscle fiber.

nodes.label: Synaptic plasticity (LTP)
nodes.summary: Activity-dependent strengthening of synapses such as long-term potentiation underlies learning and memory.

nodes.label: Neurotransmitters
nodes.summary: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers released at synapses to excite or inhibit targets.

nodes.label: Acetylcholine
nodes.summary: Acetylcholine acts at neuromuscular junctions and autonomic synapses and is degraded by acetylcholinesterase.

nodes.label: Catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine)
nodes.summary: Catecholamines derived from tyrosine mediate sympathetic and reward/motor signaling.

nodes.label: Serotonin
nodes.summary: Serotonin, derived from tryptophan, modulates mood, sleep, and appetite.

nodes.label: GABA and glycine (inhibitory)
nodes.summary: GABA and glycine are the major inhibitory neurotransmitters that hyperpolarize targets.

nodes.label: Glutamate (excitatory)
nodes.summary: Glutamate is the major excitatory neurotransmitter of the central nervous system.

nodes.label: Neuropeptides (endorphins, substance P)
nodes.summary: Neuropeptides such as endorphins and substance P act as slower neuromodulators of pain and mood.

nodes.label: Receptor determines NT effect
nodes.summary: Whether a neurotransmitter excites or inhibits depends on the postsynaptic receptor, not the transmitter alone.

nodes.label: Central Nervous System
nodes.summary: The CNS comprises the brain and spinal cord and integrates and coordinates all neural activity.

nodes.label: CNS Organization
nodes.summary: The CNS is organized into gray and white matter and protected by meninges and CSF.

nodes.label: Gray matter vs white matter
nodes.summary: Gray matter contains cell bodies while white matter contains myelinated axon tracts.

nodes.label: Meninges
nodes.summary: The dura, arachnoid, and pia mater protect the brain and spinal cord.

nodes.label: Cerebrospinal fluid
nodes.summary: CSF cushions the CNS, supplies nutrients, and removes waste from the ventricles and subarachnoid space.

nodes.label: Ventricles
nodes.summary: The brain's ventricular system holds CSF produced by the choroid plexus and ependymal cells.

nodes.label: Blood-brain barrier
nodes.summary: Tight junctions of brain capillary endothelium selectively restrict substances entering the CNS.

nodes.label: Embryonic brain divisions (3 then 5 vesicles)
nodes.summary: The neural tube forms forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, which subdivide into five secondary vesicles.

nodes.label: Brain Regions
nodes.summary: The brain divides into forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, each with specialized functions.

nodes.label: Forebrain divisions
nodes.summary: The forebrain (prosencephalon) includes the telencephalon (cerebrum) and diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus).

nodes.label: Midbrain (mesencephalon)
nodes.summary: The midbrain relays visual and auditory reflexes and contains dopaminergic motor nuclei.

nodes.label: Hindbrain (rhombencephalon)
nodes.summary: The hindbrain includes the cerebellum, pons, and medulla controlling balance and vital functions.

nodes.label: Cerebral cortex
nodes.summary: The cerebral cortex governs higher functions including perception, cognition, and voluntary movement.

nodes.label: Cortical lobes (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital)
nodes.summary: The four cortical lobes specialize in executive, somatosensory, auditory/memory, and visual functions respectively.

nodes.label: Corpus callosum
nodes.summary: The corpus callosum is the white-matter tract connecting and coordinating the two cerebral hemispheres.

nodes.label: Thalamus
nodes.summary: The thalamus is the relay station that routes sensory information (except smell) to the cortex.

nodes.label: Hypothalamus
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus maintains homeostasis and links the nervous system to the endocrine system via the pituitary.

nodes.label: Limbic system
nodes.summary: The limbic system, including the amygdala and hippocampus, governs emotion and memory.

nodes.label: Amygdala
nodes.summary: The amygdala processes fear, aggression, and the emotional salience of stimuli.

nodes.label: Hippocampus
nodes.summary: The hippocampus is essential for forming and consolidating new long-term memories.

nodes.label: Cerebellum
nodes.summary: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning.

nodes.label: Brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla)
nodes.summary: The brainstem controls vital autonomic functions such as breathing, heart rate, and reflexes.

nodes.label: Pons
nodes.summary: The pons relays signals between cortex and cerebellum and helps regulate breathing.

nodes.label: Medulla oblongata
nodes.summary: The medulla controls involuntary functions including heart rate, breathing, and blood pressure.

nodes.label: Reticular formation
nodes.summary: The reticular formation regulates arousal, sleep-wake cycles, and consciousness.

nodes.label: Basal ganglia
nodes.summary: The basal ganglia regulate the initiation and smoothing of voluntary movement.

nodes.label: Spinal Cord and Reflexes
nodes.summary: The spinal cord relays signals and mediates rapid reflex arcs.

nodes.label: Ascending and descending tracts
nodes.summary: Ascending tracts carry sensory signals up and descending tracts carry motor commands down the cord.

nodes.label: Cord gray/white arrangement
nodes.summary: In the spinal cord gray matter is central (butterfly shape) and white matter is peripheral, opposite the brain.

nodes.label: Dorsal and ventral roots
nodes.summary: Sensory fibers enter via the dorsal root and motor fibers exit via the ventral root.

nodes.label: Reflex arc
nodes.summary: A reflex arc links a sensory receptor to an effector through the spinal cord with minimal brain involvement.

nodes.label: Monosynaptic reflex (stretch/knee-jerk)
nodes.summary: The monosynaptic stretch reflex involves one synapse between a sensory and a motor neuron.

nodes.label: Polysynaptic reflex (withdrawal)
nodes.summary: Polysynaptic reflexes such as withdrawal include one or more interneurons between sensory and motor neurons.

nodes.label: Peripheral Nervous System
nodes.summary: The PNS connects the CNS to the rest of the body via somatic and autonomic divisions.

nodes.label: PNS Divisions
nodes.summary: The PNS splits into sensory/somatic and autonomic (involuntary) divisions.

nodes.label: Somatic nervous system
nodes.summary: The somatic nervous system controls voluntary skeletal muscle movement and conveys sensation.

nodes.label: Autonomic nervous system
nodes.summary: The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions of smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands.

nodes.label: Afferent vs efferent divisions
nodes.summary: The afferent (sensory) division carries input to the CNS and the efferent (motor) division carries output away.

nodes.label: Cranial and spinal nerves
nodes.summary: Twelve pairs of cranial nerves and thirty-one pairs of spinal nerves carry PNS information.

nodes.label: Ganglia (PNS cell-body clusters)
nodes.summary: Ganglia are clusters of neuronal cell bodies located outside the CNS in the peripheral nervous system.

nodes.label: Autonomic Subdivisions
nodes.summary: The autonomic system divides into antagonistic sympathetic and parasympathetic branches.

nodes.label: Sympathetic (fight-or-flight)
nodes.summary: The sympathetic division mobilizes the body for stress, raising heart rate and dilating pupils.

nodes.label: Parasympathetic (rest-and-digest)
nodes.summary: The parasympathetic division conserves energy, slowing heart rate and promoting digestion.

nodes.label: Dual innervation and antagonism
nodes.summary: Most visceral organs receive opposing sympathetic and parasympathetic input that finely tunes function.

nodes.label: Sympathetic thoracolumbar outflow
nodes.summary: Sympathetic fibers originate from thoracolumbar spinal segments with short pre- and long postganglionic neurons.

nodes.label: Parasympathetic craniosacral outflow
nodes.summary: Parasympathetic fibers originate from cranial and sacral regions with long pre- and short postganglionic neurons.

nodes.label: Vagus nerve
nodes.summary: The vagus nerve carries most parasympathetic output to the thoracic and abdominal viscera.

nodes.label: Autonomic neurotransmitters/receptors
nodes.summary: All preganglionic and parasympathetic postganglionic fibers release ACh; most sympathetic postganglionic fibers release norepinephrine.

nodes.label: Cholinergic and adrenergic receptors
nodes.summary: Nicotinic and muscarinic cholinergic receptors and alpha/beta adrenergic receptors mediate autonomic effects.

nodes.label: Adrenal medulla as sympathetic effector
nodes.summary: The adrenal medulla acts as a modified sympathetic ganglion releasing epinephrine into the blood.

nodes.label: Enteric nervous system
nodes.summary: The enteric nervous system is a semi-autonomous gut network that controls digestion largely independently.

nodes.label: Endocrine System
nodes.summary: The endocrine system uses hormones secreted into the blood to regulate distant target organs.

nodes.label: Hormone Principles
nodes.summary: Hormones act on target cells bearing specific receptors and are regulated by feedback.

nodes.label: Definition of a hormone
nodes.summary: A hormone is a chemical messenger secreted into the blood that acts on distant target cells.

nodes.label: Endocrine, paracrine, autocrine signaling
nodes.summary: Endocrine acts at a distance, paracrine on neighbors, and autocrine on the secreting cell itself.

nodes.label: Target-cell specificity (receptors)
nodes.summary: A hormone affects only cells expressing the matching receptor, so the same hormone can have tissue-specific effects.

nodes.label: Peptide hormones
nodes.summary: Water-soluble peptide hormones bind surface receptors and act via second messengers with rapid, short-lived effects.

nodes.label: Steroid hormones
nodes.summary: Lipid-soluble steroid hormones cross the membrane to bind intracellular receptors that alter gene transcription.

nodes.label: Amino-acid-derived hormones
nodes.summary: Tyrosine-derived hormones (catecholamines, thyroid hormone) vary between peptide-like and steroid-like behavior.

nodes.label: Second-messenger cascades
nodes.summary: Peptide hormones often act through cAMP, IP3/DAG, or kinase cascades that amplify the signal.

nodes.label: Signal amplification
nodes.summary: A single hormone-receptor binding event can activate many enzymes, greatly amplifying the cellular response.

nodes.label: Hormone transport proteins
nodes.summary: Lipid-soluble hormones travel bound to plasma carrier proteins because they are insoluble in blood.

nodes.label: Solubility, onset, and duration
nodes.summary: Peptide hormones act fast and briefly; steroid hormones act slowly but produce durable, long-lasting effects.

nodes.label: Negative feedback regulation
nodes.summary: Most hormone axes are controlled by negative feedback that inhibits further release when levels rise.

nodes.label: Positive feedback (e.g., oxytocin, LH surge)
nodes.summary: Positive feedback amplifies a response, as in oxytocin during labor and the LH surge before ovulation.

nodes.label: Endocrine axes (multi-level feedback)
nodes.summary: Hypothalamic-pituitary-target axes use multi-level negative feedback in which the end hormone inhibits upstream release.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus and pituitary form the master control center coordinating endocrine output.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus secretes releasing and inhibiting hormones that control the anterior pituitary.

nodes.label: Hypophyseal portal system
nodes.summary: A portal blood system carries hypothalamic hormones directly to the anterior pituitary.

nodes.label: Anterior pituitary hormones
nodes.summary: The anterior pituitary secretes FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, prolactin, and growth hormone (FLAT PEG).

nodes.label: Growth hormone
nodes.summary: Growth hormone promotes growth and metabolism, acting partly via IGF-1 from the liver.

nodes.label: ACTH
nodes.summary: ACTH stimulates the adrenal cortex to release cortisol.

nodes.label: TSH
nodes.summary: TSH stimulates the thyroid to produce and release thyroid hormone.

nodes.label: Prolactin
nodes.summary: Prolactin from the anterior pituitary stimulates milk production and is tonically inhibited by dopamine.

nodes.label: Gonadotropins (FSH and LH)
nodes.summary: FSH and LH from the anterior pituitary regulate gametogenesis and gonadal sex-hormone secretion.

nodes.label: Tropic vs direct hormones
nodes.summary: Tropic hormones stimulate other endocrine glands while direct hormones act on non-endocrine targets.

nodes.label: Posterior pituitary hormones
nodes.summary: The posterior pituitary stores and releases hypothalamus-made ADH and oxytocin.

nodes.label: Posterior pituitary is neural tissue
nodes.summary: The posterior pituitary is a downgrowth of neural tissue that stores hormones made by hypothalamic neurons.

nodes.label: ADH (vasopressin)
nodes.summary: ADH increases water reabsorption in the kidney collecting ducts to conserve water.

nodes.label: Oxytocin
nodes.summary: Oxytocin stimulates uterine contraction during labor and milk ejection during nursing.

nodes.label: Peripheral Endocrine Glands
nodes.summary: Peripheral glands secrete hormones regulating metabolism, calcium, stress, and reproduction.

nodes.label: Thyroid hormone (T3/T4)
nodes.summary: Thyroid hormone raises basal metabolic rate and is required for normal development.

nodes.label: Calcitonin
nodes.summary: Calcitonin from the thyroid lowers blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts.

nodes.label: Parathyroid hormone (PTH)
nodes.summary: PTH raises blood calcium by activating osteoclasts, increasing renal reabsorption, and activating vitamin D.

nodes.label: Vitamin D (calcitriol)
nodes.summary: Active vitamin D raises blood calcium by increasing intestinal calcium absorption.

nodes.label: Adrenal cortex (cortisol, aldosterone, sex steroids)
nodes.summary: The adrenal cortex secretes glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and androgens.

nodes.label: Cortisol
nodes.summary: Cortisol is a stress glucocorticoid that raises blood glucose and suppresses immune function.

nodes.label: Aldosterone
nodes.summary: Aldosterone promotes renal Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion, raising blood pressure.

nodes.label: Adrenal androgens
nodes.summary: The adrenal cortex secretes weak androgens (e.g., DHEA) that contribute to secondary sex characteristics.

nodes.label: Adrenal medulla (epinephrine/norepinephrine)
nodes.summary: The adrenal medulla releases epinephrine and norepinephrine to amplify the fight-or-flight response.

nodes.label: Insulin (beta cells)
nodes.summary: Insulin from pancreatic beta cells lowers blood glucose by promoting uptake and storage.

nodes.label: Glucagon (alpha cells)
nodes.summary: Glucagon from pancreatic alpha cells raises blood glucose via glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Somatostatin (delta cells)
nodes.summary: Somatostatin from pancreatic delta cells inhibits insulin and glucagon secretion.

nodes.label: Pancreas as endocrine and exocrine gland
nodes.summary: The pancreas has endocrine islets of Langerhans and exocrine acini that secrete digestive enzymes.

nodes.label: Gonadal hormones
nodes.summary: The testes secrete testosterone and the ovaries secrete estrogen and progesterone.

nodes.label: Pineal gland (melatonin)
nodes.summary: The pineal gland secretes melatonin to regulate circadian and sleep-wake rhythms.

nodes.label: Thymus (thymosin)
nodes.summary: The thymus secretes thymosin that supports T-lymphocyte maturation.

nodes.label: Atrial natriuretic peptide
nodes.summary: ANP from the heart promotes sodium and water excretion to lower blood pressure.

nodes.label: Kidney hormones (EPO, renin)
nodes.summary: The kidney secretes erythropoietin to stimulate red-cell production and renin to initiate the RAAS pathway.

nodes.label: GI hormones (gastrin, secretin, CCK)
nodes.summary: The digestive tract secretes gastrin, secretin, and cholecystokinin to regulate digestion.

nodes.label: Coordination and Homeostasis
nodes.summary: The nervous and endocrine systems integrate to maintain homeostasis across organ systems.

nodes.label: Nervous vs Endocrine Control
nodes.summary: Nervous and endocrine systems differ in speed, duration, and breadth of their signals.

nodes.label: Speed vs duration tradeoff
nodes.summary: Neural signals are fast and brief while hormonal signals are slower but longer-lasting and widespread.

nodes.label: Neuroendocrine integration
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus and adrenal medulla illustrate direct coupling of neural and endocrine signaling.

nodes.label: Homeostatic Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Feedback loops keep internal variables within set ranges.

nodes.label: Set point and feedback loops
nodes.summary: Homeostasis maintains a regulated variable near a set point using sensors, integrators, and effectors.

nodes.label: Thermoregulation
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus regulates body temperature via shivering, sweating, and vasomotor responses.

nodes.label: Blood glucose regulation
nodes.summary: Insulin and glucagon act antagonistically to keep blood glucose within a narrow range.

nodes.label: Calcium homeostasis
nodes.summary: PTH and calcitonin act antagonistically to keep blood calcium within a tight range.

nodes.label: Osmoregulation and blood pressure
nodes.summary: ADH and aldosterone regulate water and electrolyte balance to control blood pressure and osmolarity.

nodes.label: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
nodes.summary: Low blood pressure triggers renin release, generating angiotensin II and aldosterone to restore volume and pressure.

nodes.label: Integrated stress response
nodes.summary: Stress activates both sympathetic output and the HPA axis to mobilize energy and prepare the body.

nodes.label: HPA axis
nodes.summary: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis releases CRH, ACTH, and cortisol to mediate the slower stress response.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Active transport / membrane pumps using ATP
Selective membrane permeability and ion channels
Electrochemical / membrane potential and the Nernst equation
Electrochemistry of concentration cells / multi-ion potentials
Peptide / amino acid structure and polarity
G-protein-coupled receptors and cAMP signal transduction
Tyrosine amino acid as a biosynthetic precursor
Bone remodeling by osteoclasts and osteoblasts
Nephron function and renal regulation of blood volume
Digestive tract structure and secretion control
